context.description: A philosophical book passage argues that willing and self-understanding depend on an underlying symbolic world shaped by internal consistency. It uses flat-Earth belief as an example to explain how a person's worldview can remain coherent even when it conflicts with another person's reasoning. The text asks whether one can consciously change one's own symbolic world and desire new meanings or gods.
normalization_notes: Translated German prose into English retrieval summary.
Book title inferred from source file name.

rows.paragraph_text: The author asks whether conscious change of one's symbolic world is possible, given that willing arises from unconscious premises.
rows.text_content: The author asks whether conscious change of one's symbolic world is possible, given that willing arises from unconscious premises.

rows.paragraph_text: A flat-Earth belief is used to show that a worldview can be internally consistent even when it appears irrational to outsiders.
rows.text_content: A flat-Earth belief is used to show that a worldview can be internally consistent even when it appears irrational to outsiders.

rows.paragraph_text: The passage concludes that understanding such beliefs requires recognizing the symbolic logic that makes them seem legitimate to the believer.
rows.text_content: The passage concludes that understanding such beliefs requires recognizing the symbolic logic that makes them seem legitimate to the believer.

content.free_text: book_page nonfiction_philosophy philosophy_of_consciousness Philosophical prose on consciousness, symbolic world, internal consistency, and worldview change. Flat-Earth belief is used as the main example.
